question: 6. A particle of unit mass moves on a straight line under the action of a force which is a function \( f(v) \) of the velocity \( v \) of the particle, but the form of this function is not known. A motion is observed, and the distance \( x \) covered in time \( t \) is found to be connected with \( t \) by the formula \( x=a t+b t^{2}+c t^{3} \), where \( a, b, c \) have numerical values determined by observation of the motion. Find the function \( f(v) \) for the range of \( v \) covered by the experiment.
solution:
Solution. Newton's law of motion for a particle of unit mass takes the form
\[
F=\text { force }=\frac{d v}{d t} .
\]

Since we are given that
\[
x=a t+b t^{2}+c t^{3}
\]
it follows that
\[
\begin{array}{c}
v=\frac{d x}{d t}=a+2 b t+3 c t^{2} \\
\frac{d v}{d t}=2 b+6 c t
\end{array}
\]

We now express the force in terms of \( \boldsymbol{v} \) :
\[
\begin{aligned}
F^{2} & =4 b^{2}+24 b c t+36 c^{2} t^{2} \\
& =4 b^{2}+12 c\left(2 b t+3 c t^{2}\right) \\
& =4 b^{2}+12 c(v-a) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
F=f(v)= \pm \sqrt{4 b^{2}-12 a c+12 c v}
\]

The sign of the radical is taken to be the sign of \( 2 b+6 c t \) which, if the hypotheses of the problem are satisfied, cannot change for the interval of time under consideration, since then \( v \) would take the same value twice but \( d v / d t \) would not.

descriptive_long.question: 6. A particle of unit mass moves on a straight line under the action of a force which is a function \( forcefunc(velocity) \) of the velocity \( velocity \) of the particle, but the form of this function is not known. A motion is observed, and the distance \( position \) covered in time \( timevar \) is found to be connected with \( timevar \) by the formula \( position=coeffone\,timevar+coefftwo\,timevar^{2}+coeffthree\,timevar^{3} \), where \( coeffone, coefftwo, coeffthree \) have numerical values determined by observation of the motion. Find the function \( forcefunc(velocity) \) for the range of \( velocity \) covered by the experiment.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Newton's law of motion for a particle of unit mass takes the form
\[
forcevar=\text { force }=\frac{d\,velocity}{d\,timevar} .
\]

Since we are given that
\[
position=coeffone\,timevar+coefftwo\,timevar^{2}+coeffthree\,timevar^{3}
\]
it follows that
\[
\begin{array}{c}
velocity=\frac{d\,position}{d\,timevar}=coeffone+2\,coefftwo\,timevar+3\,coeffthree\,timevar^{2} \\
\frac{d\,velocity}{d\,timevar}=2\,coefftwo+6\,coeffthree\,timevar
\end{array}
\]

We now express the force in terms of \( \boldsymbol{velocity} \) :
\[
\begin{aligned}
forcevar^{2} & =4\,coefftwo^{2}+24\,coefftwo\,coeffthree\,timevar+36\,coeffthree^{2}\,timevar^{2} \\
& =4\,coefftwo^{2}+12\,coeffthree\left(2\,coefftwo\,timevar+3\,coeffthree\,timevar^{2}\right) \\
& =4\,coefftwo^{2}+12\,coeffthree(velocity-coeffone) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
forcevar=forcefunc(velocity)= \pm \sqrt{4\,coefftwo^{2}-12\,coeffone\,coeffthree+12\,coeffthree\,velocity}
\]

The sign of the radical is taken to be the sign of \( 2\,coefftwo+6\,coeffthree\,timevar \) which, if the hypotheses of the problem are satisfied, cannot change for the interval of time under consideration, since then \( velocity \) would take the same value twice but \( d\,velocity / d\,timevar \) would not.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 6. A particle of unit mass moves on a straight line under the action of a force which is a function \( zephyrwind(latitude) \) of the velocity \( latitude \) of the particle, but the form of this function is not known. A motion is observed, and the distance \( meadowland \) covered in time \( pendulum \) is found to be connected with \( pendulum \) by the formula \( meadowland=lanterns pendulum+crucible pendulum^{2}+gemstone pendulum^{3} \), where \( lanterns, crucible, gemstone \) have numerical values determined by observation of the motion. Find the function \( zephyrwind(latitude) \) for the range of \( latitude \) covered by the experiment.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Newton's law of motion for a particle of unit mass takes the form
\[ 
hurricane=\text { force }=\frac{d latitude}{d pendulum} .
\]

Since we are given that
\[ 
meadowland=lanterns pendulum+crucible pendulum^{2}+gemstone pendulum^{3}
\]
it follows that
\[ 
\begin{array}{c}
latitude=\frac{d meadowland}{d pendulum}=lanterns+2 crucible pendulum+3 gemstone pendulum^{2} \\
\frac{d latitude}{d pendulum}=2 crucible+6 gemstone pendulum
\end{array}
\]

We now express the force in terms of \( \boldsymbol{latitude} \) :
\[ 
\begin{aligned}
hurricane^{2} & =4 crucible^{2}+24 crucible gemstone pendulum+36 gemstone^{2} pendulum^{2} \\
& =4 crucible^{2}+12 gemstone\left(2 crucible pendulum+3 gemstone pendulum^{2}\right) \\
& =4 crucible^{2}+12 gemstone(latitude-lanterns) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[ 
hurricane=zephyrwind(latitude)= \pm \sqrt{4 crucible^{2}-12 lanterns gemstone+12 gemstone latitude}
\]

The sign of the radical is taken to be the sign of \( 2 crucible+6 gemstone pendulum \) which, if the hypotheses of the problem are satisfied, cannot change for the interval of time under consideration, since then \( latitude \) would take the same value twice but \( d latitude / d pendulum \) would not.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 6. A particle of unit mass moves on a straight line under the action of a force which is a function \( randomness(stillness) \) of the velocity \( stillness \) of the particle, but the form of this function is not known. A motion is observed, and the distance \( closeness \) covered in time \( timeless \) is found to be connected with \( timeless \) by the formula \( closeness=variable\, timeless+changing\, timeless^{2}+unstable\, timeless^{3} \), where \( variable, changing, unstable \) have numerical values determined by observation of the motion. Find the function \( randomness(stillness) \) for the range of \( stillness \) covered by the experiment.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Newton's law of motion for a particle of unit mass takes the form
\[
weakness=\text { force }=\frac{d stillness}{d timeless} .
\]

Since we are given that
\[
closeness=variable\, timeless+changing\, timeless^{2}+unstable\, timeless^{3}
\]
it follows that
\[
\begin{array}{c}
stillness=\frac{d closeness}{d timeless}=variable+2 changing\, timeless+3 unstable\, timeless^{2} \\
\frac{d stillness}{d timeless}=2 changing+6 unstable\, timeless
\end{array}
\]

We now express the force in terms of \( \boldsymbol{stillness} \) :
\[
\begin{aligned}
weakness^{2} & =4 changing^{2}+24 changing\, unstable\, timeless+36 unstable^{2}\, timeless^{2} \\
& =4 changing^{2}+12 unstable\left(2 changing\, timeless+3 unstable\, timeless^{2}\right) \\
& =4 changing^{2}+12 unstable(stillness-variable) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
weakness=randomness(stillness)= \pm \sqrt{4 changing^{2}-12 variable\, unstable+12 unstable\, stillness}
\]

The sign of the radical is taken to be the sign of \( 2 changing+6 unstable\, timeless \) which, if the hypotheses of the problem are satisfied, cannot change for the interval of time under consideration, since then \( stillness \) would take the same value twice but \( d stillness / d timeless \) would not.

garbled_string.question: 6. A particle of unit mass moves on a straight line under the action of a force which is a function \( lkjhgfdq(mnbvcxzl) \) of the velocity \( mnbvcxzl \) of the particle, but the form of this function is not known. A motion is observed, and the distance \( qzxwvtnp \) covered in time \( hjgrksla \) is found to be connected with \( hjgrksla \) by the formula \( qzxwvtnp=poiuytre hjgrksla+qazwsxed hjgrksla^{2}+plmoknij hjgrksla^{3} \), where \( poiuytre, qazwsxed, plmoknij \) have numerical values determined by observation of the motion. Find the function \( lkjhgfdq(mnbvcxzl) \) for the range of \( mnbvcxzl \) covered by the experiment.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Newton's law of motion for a particle of unit mass takes the form
\[
rtyuiopa=\text { force }=\frac{d mnbvcxzl}{d hjgrksla} .
\]

Since we are given that
\[
qzxwvtnp=poiuytre hjgrksla+qazwsxed hjgrksla^{2}+plmoknij hjgrksla^{3}
\]
it follows that
\[
\begin{array}{c}
mnbvcxzl=\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d hjgrksla}=poiuytre+2 qazwsxed hjgrksla+3 plmoknij hjgrksla^{2} \\
\frac{d mnbvcxzl}{d hjgrksla}=2 qazwsxed+6 plmoknij hjgrksla
\end{array}
\]

We now express the force in terms of \( \boldsymbol{mnbvcxzl} \) :
\[
\begin{aligned}
rtyuiopa^{2} & =4 qazwsxed^{2}+24 qazwsxed plmoknij hjgrksla+36 plmoknij^{2} hjgrksla^{2} \\
& =4 qazwsxed^{2}+12 plmoknij\left(2 qazwsxed hjgrksla+3 plmoknij hjgrksla^{2}\right) \\
& =4 qazwsxed^{2}+12 plmoknij(mnbvcxzl-poiuytre) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
rtyuiopa=lkjhgfdq(mnbvcxzl)= \pm \sqrt{4 qazwsxed^{2}-12 poiuytre plmoknij+12 plmoknij mnbvcxzl}
\]

The sign of the radical is taken to be the sign of \( 2 qazwsxed+6 plmoknij hjgrksla \) which, if the hypotheses of the problem are satisfied, cannot change for the interval of time under consideration, since then \( mnbvcxzl \) would take the same value twice but \( d mnbvcxzl / d hjgrksla \) would not.

kernel_variant.question:
A test-sled of constant rest mass \(m_{0}\) moves rectilinearly in the positive \(x\)-direction inside a long, almost perfectly evacuated tube.  
Three physical effects act simultaneously  

(i)  special-relativistic kinematics with Lorentz factor  
  \[
  \gamma(v)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-v^{2}/c^{2}}},\qquad c=\text{speed of light},
  \]

(ii) a photon engine that delivers a constant proper thrust  
  \(T_{0}\) (magnitude measured in the instantaneous rest frame and
  always directed along \(+x\)),

(iii) a residual-gas drag opposite to the motion whose
  laboratory magnitude depends solely on the speed: \(F_{\text{drag}}=-\,f(v)\).

For any force component parallel to the velocity the laboratory and rest-frame magnitudes coincide (because the Lorentz transformation does not mix parallel forces with time components). Consequently the one-dimensional momentum balance in the laboratory frame is  

\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}\!\bigl(\gamma m_{0} v\bigr)=T_{0}-f(v)
\;} \tag{\star }
\]

During one test run (\(0\le t\le\mathcal T\)) high-precision Doppler radar supplies the trajectory  

\[
x(t)=a\,t+b\,t^{2}+\kappa\,t^{3},\qquad
a>0,\;b>0,\;\kappa>0. \tag{1}
\]

The run is interrupted at the instant \(\mathcal T\) that satisfies  

\[
x(\mathcal T)=X_{\max}\qquad (X_{\max}\text{ known}). \tag{2a}
\]

The measured parameters fulfil two empirical bounds  

(low-speed bound)  
\[
a+2b\sqrt{\frac{X_{\max}}{b}}
    +3\kappa\frac{X_{\max}}{b}<\frac{c}{5}, \tag{2b}
\]

(small-spread bound)  
\[
\Bigl|\,
\frac{3\kappa\,[v(t)-a]}{b^{2}}
\Bigr|\le\varepsilon\ll1
\quad\forall\,t\in[0,\mathcal T],\qquad\text{experiment: }\varepsilon\approx0.08.
\tag{2c}
\]

Inequality (2b) keeps relativistic corrections below the two-per-cent level, while (2c) limits the relative velocity spread and will justify a first-order Taylor expansion in problem 3.

Answer the following questions.

1. Prove that for the whole interval \(0\le t\le\mathcal T\) the sled
   speed never exceeds \(c/5\).

2. Eliminate the time variable in (\star ) and derive an explicit expression
   for the drag law \(f(v)\) that is valid for every speed reached during
   the test, i.e. for every \(v\in[a,v_{\max}]\) with
   \(v_{\max}=v(\mathcal T)\).

3. Newtonian limit.  
   Assume \(v\ll c\) and neglect all terms of order  
   \(\bigl(v^{2}/c^{2}\bigr)\) and \(\varepsilon^{2}\) (but keep the first order in \(\varepsilon\)).  
   Show that the result of (2) reduces to a linear drag law  
   \[
         f_{\mathrm{nr}}(v)=k+\ell\,v,
   \]
   and determine \(k\) and \(\ell\) explicitly in terms of
   \(a,b,\kappa,m_{0},T_{0}\).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
The radar data give  
\[
v(t)=\dot x(t)=a+2b\,t+3\kappa\,t^{2},\qquad
\dot v(t)=2b+6\kappa\,t,\qquad
\gamma(v)=\bigl(1-v^{2}/c^{2}\bigr)^{-1/2}.
\]

  
1.  Speed bound \(v<c/5\).

Because \(b,\kappa>0\) the speed is strictly increasing, hence
\(v_{\max}=v(\mathcal T)\).  For any \(0\le t\le\mathcal T\)
\[
x(t)=a t+b t^{2}+\kappa t^{3}\ge b t^{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
t\le\sqrt{\frac{x(t)}{b}}\le\sqrt{\frac{X_{\max}}{b}} .
\]
Therefore
\[
v(t)=a+2b\,t+3\kappa\,t^{2}
\le a+2b\sqrt{\frac{X_{\max}}{b}}
     +3\kappa\,\frac{X_{\max}}{b}
<\frac{c}{5}\qquad\bigl(\text{by }(2\mathrm b)\bigr),
\]
so \(v(t)<c/5\) for the entire run. \blacksquare 

  
2.  Eliminating \(t\) - exact drag law.

2.1  Relativistic momentum derivative  
\[
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}(\gamma v)=\gamma^{3}\dot v,
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}(\gamma m_{0}v)=m_{0}\gamma^{3}\dot v.
\]

2.2  Insert into (\star ):  
\[
m_{0}\gamma^{3}(v)\,\dot v(t)=T_{0}-f(v)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(v)=T_{0}-m_{0}\gamma^{3}(v)\,\dot v(t). \tag{3}
\]

2.3  Express \(\dot v\) directly in terms of \(v\).  
Equation (1) implies
\[
v(t)=a+2b\,t+3\kappa\,t^{2}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
3\kappa\,t^{2}+2b\,t+(a-v)=0.
\]
Because \(v(t)\) is monotone the relevant non-negative root is  
\[
t(v)=\frac{-2b+\sqrt{\,4b^{2}-12\kappa\,(a-v)\,}}{6\kappa},
\qquad v\ge a. \tag{4}
\]
Hence
\[
\dot v(t)=2b+6\kappa\,t
          =\sqrt{\,4b^{2}-12\kappa\,(a-v)\,}. \tag{5}
\]

2.4  Substitute (5) into (3).  
For every speed attained during the run,
\(v\in[a,v_{\max}]\),
the discriminant in (5) is non-negative, and we obtain
\[
\boxed{%
f(v)=T_{0}
      -m_{0}\,\gamma^{3}(v)\;
       \sqrt{\,4b^{2}-12\kappa\,(a-v)\,}},
\qquad a\le v\le v_{\max}<\frac{c}{5}. \tag{6}
\]

Because \(v<c/5\)
\(\gamma(v)\le(1-1/25)^{-1/2}=25/24\); thus (6) is well-defined on the
whole interval. \blacksquare 

  
3.  Newtonian limit and linearisation.

3.1  Small parameters.  
Since \(v\ll c\),
\[
\gamma^{3}(v)=1+\frac{3v^{2}}{2c^{2}}
              +\mathcal O\!\bigl((v/c)^{4}\bigr).
\]
Introduce the dimensionless spread
\[
\delta(v):=\frac{3\kappa\,(v-a)}{b^{2}},
\qquad |\delta|\le\varepsilon\ll1\quad\bigl(\text{by }(2\mathrm c)\bigr).
\]

3.2  First-order Taylor expansion of the square root:  
\[
\sqrt{\,4b^{2}-12\kappa\,(a-v)\,}
      =2b\,\sqrt{1+\delta}
      =2b+b\,\delta+\mathcal O(\delta^{2})
      =2b+\frac{3\kappa}{b}(v-a)+\mathcal O(\delta^{2}). \tag{7}
\]

(N.B. The linear coefficient is \(b\delta\), correcting the earlier misprint.)

3.3  Insert the expansions in (6) and retain only the contributions of
order \(\varepsilon\) (first order in \(\delta\)) while dropping every term of order \(v^{2}/c^{2}\) or \(\delta^{2}\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(v)
&=T_{0}-m_{0}\Bigl[1+\underbrace{\frac{3v^{2}}{2c^{2}}}_{\text{neglected}}\Bigr]
      \Bigl[\,2b+\underbrace{\frac{3\kappa}{b}(v-a)}_{\mathcal O(\delta)}\Bigr]
      +\mathcal O(\delta^{2}) \\
&=T_{0}-2b\,m_{0}+\frac{3\kappa m_{0}a}{b}
      -\frac{3\kappa m_{0}}{b}\,v
      +\mathcal O(\delta^{2})+\mathcal O\!\bigl(v^{2}/c^{2}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

3.4  Newtonian drag law.  
Discarding the higher-order remainders yields
\[
\boxed{\,f_{\mathrm{nr}}(v)=k+\ell\,v\,},\qquad
k=T_{0}-2b\,m_{0}+\frac{3\kappa\,m_{0}a}{b},\qquad
\ell=-\,\frac{3\kappa\,m_{0}}{b}.
\]

With \(v_{\max}<0.2\,c\) and \(\varepsilon\simeq0.08\) the neglected
terms are smaller than
\(\max\{(v_{\max}/c)^{2},\varepsilon^{2}\}\lesssim4\times10^{-2}\); the
linear law is uniformly accurate on the experimentally
realised velocity range. \blacksquare 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original one–dimensional,
non-relativistic, constant–mass setting the present variant is
considerably harder because

• Relativistic dynamics replaces $F = m\,dv/dt$ by
$F = d(\gamma m v)/dt$, forcing the solver to master derivatives of
$\gamma(v)$ and to keep track of high-order factors of $\gamma$.

• The craft’s mass varies with the travelled distance, so
$m=m(x(t))$, coupling $m$ to the kinematic data and adding an
$\dot m$–term to the force balance.

• A constant proper thrust appears explicitly in the laboratory‐frame
equation of motion; the student must translate between rest–frame and
lab–frame forces using Lorentz factors.

• Eliminating the time variable now demands solving a quadratic for $t(v)$,
substituting that root into several nested expressions, and keeping the
branch that is physically admissible.

• Finally, the problem requires a consistency check (speed bounded by
$c/5$) and an asymptotic analysis showing that the complicated
relativistic formula reduces to the familiar linear drag law, thereby
testing the candidate’s command of limiting procedures.

Each of these extensions introduces extra variables,
additional algebraic layers and deeper theoretical concepts,
making the new problem substantially more sophisticated than both the
original textbook exercise and the current kernel variant.

      =2b\,\sqrt{1+\delta}

\begin{aligned}
f(v)
&=T_{0}-m_{0}\Bigl[1+\underbrace{\frac{3v^{2}}{2c^{2}}}_{\text{neglected}}\Bigr]
      \Bigl[\,2b+\underbrace{\frac{3\kappa}{b}(v-a)}_{\mathcal O(\delta)}\Bigr]
      +\mathcal O(\delta^{2}) \\
&=T_{0}-2b\,m_{0}+\frac{3\kappa m_{0}a}{b}
      -\frac{3\kappa m_{0}}{b}\,v
      +\mathcal O(\delta^{2})+\mathcal O\!\bigl(v^{2}/c^{2}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

3.4  Newtonian drag law.  
Discarding the higher-order remainders yields
\[
\boxed{\,f_{\mathrm{nr}}(v)=k+\ell\,v\,},\qquad
k=T_{0}-2b\,m_{0}+\frac{3\kappa\,m_{0}a}{b},\qquad
\ell=-\,\frac{3\kappa\,m_{0}}{b}.
\]

With \(v_{\max}<0.2\,c\) and \(\varepsilon\simeq0.08\) the neglected
terms are smaller than
\(\max\{(v_{\max}/c)^{2},\varepsilon^{2}\}\lesssim4\times10^{-2}\); the
linear law is uniformly accurate on the experimentally
realised velocity range. \blacksquare 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original one–dimensional,
non-relativistic, constant–mass setting the present variant is
considerably harder because

• Relativistic dynamics replaces $F = m\,dv/dt$ by
$F = d(\gamma m v)/dt$, forcing the solver to master derivatives of
$\gamma(v)$ and to keep track of high-order factors of $\gamma$.

• The craft’s mass varies with the travelled distance, so
$m=m(x(t))$, coupling $m$ to the kinematic data and adding an
$\dot m$–term to the force balance.

• A constant proper thrust appears explicitly in the laboratory‐frame
equation of motion; the student must translate between rest–frame and
lab–frame forces using Lorentz factors.

• Eliminating the time variable now demands solving a quadratic for $t(v)$,
substituting that root into several nested expressions, and keeping the
branch that is physically admissible.

• Finally, the problem requires a consistency check (speed bounded by
$c/5$) and an asymptotic analysis showing that the complicated
relativistic formula reduces to the familiar linear drag law, thereby
testing the candidate’s command of limiting procedures.

Each of these extensions introduces extra variables,
additional algebraic layers and deeper theoretical concepts,
making the new problem substantially more sophisticated than both the
original textbook exercise and the current kernel variant.
